findings.title: generateRunId has only ~16 bits of entropy in its random suffix
findings.description: Line 19: `crypto.randomBytes(2).toString('hex')` produces a 4-character hex suffix (16 bits, ~65k space). Combined with a second-precision timestamp (line 18), two run-state writes within the same wall-clock second have a non-trivial collision probability (birthday-style: ~1% at ~36 concurrent runs). On collision, `saveRunState` (line 26) overwrites without checking, silently destroying the prior run's state. Not a security issue — runIds are not used for authentication or authorization, and `crypto.randomBytes` is fine for non-security uses — but it is a data-loss bug if a user fans out many sandboxes.
findings.recommendation: Use 4–6 random bytes (8–12 hex chars) for the suffix and/or check existence before write; fail loudly on collision instead of overwriting.

revalidation.reasoning: I read state.ts fully and checked the detached-run call path in sandbox/orchestrator.ts where generateRunId and saveRunState are used. The current code no longer matches the finding: generateRunId uses `crypto.randomBytes(8).toString("hex")`, giving a 16-character hex suffix with 64 bits of entropy, not the reported 2 random bytes / 16 bits. saveRunState has also been hardened to use `fs.openSync(dest, "wx")`, so even an unexpected runId collision fails loudly instead of silently overwriting an existing state file. runsDir still goes through dataDir(projectId), which validates the projectId segment in @deepsec/core paths.ts. I could not inspect git history because this checkout is not a git repository, but the current source contains both recommended mitigations: substantially more entropy and exclusive file creation. The original data-loss bug is therefore patched in the current code.
